title: AI/ML Systems Thinking for Staff+ Engineers at FAANG
description: As a Staff+ engineer at FAANG, you will work alongside ML engineers and data scientists, design ML-powered systems, and make architectural decisions about AI infrastructure. This article covers ML system architecture patterns, working with ML teams, MLOps and ML infrastructure, LLM integration patterns, evaluation and monitoring of ML systems, and how SAP engineers can bridge their data experience to ML systems thinking at FAANG.

sections.title: Why Staff+ Engineers Need ML Systems Thinking

description.value: Five years ago, an infrastructure Staff+ engineer could build a successful career designing databases, distributed systems, and deployment pipelines without ever touching machine learning. That world is gone. Every FAANG company now has ML components embedded in nearly every product. Google Search, YouTube recommendations, Gmail smart compose, and Google Ads are fundamentally ML systems. Meta's news feed, ad ranking, and content moderation are ML-driven. Amazon's supply chain, product recommendations, and Alexa are ML systems. Apple's photos, keyboard, and Siri all depend on ML.

As a Staff+ engineer, you do not need to train models. You need to design the systems that train, serve, monitor, and iterate on models. You need to understand the failure modes of ML systems — data drift, training-serving skew, feature staleness, model cascading failures — because these are system-level problems, not ML problems. An ML engineer optimizes loss functions. A Staff+ systems engineer optimizes the pipeline that makes loss function optimization possible at scale. If you cannot distinguish between a model error and a data pipeline error in an incident, you cannot be the effective on-call escalation for an ML-powered service.

For SAP engineers, this represents a massive opportunity. SAP engineers understand structured data, transactional integrity, data governance, and pipeline reliability better than most FAANG engineers. The ML industry is struggling with data quality, lineage tracking, and governance — problems that SAP has solved for decades. Your experience with SAP's data models, ETL pipelines, and master data management gives you a unique lens on ML data infrastructure. The bridge is not from SAP to ML model building — it is from SAP data engineering to ML data infrastructure, feature stores, and data quality monitoring.

ML systems at FAANG follow predictable architectural patterns. The training infrastructure includes data ingestion pipelines, feature computation, model training, and model evaluation. The serving infrastructure includes model loading, inference requests, feature retrieval, and prediction post-processing. The critical insight for Staff+ engineers: the serving path is a real-time system with strict latency requirements (typically <50ms p99 for online inference), while the training path is a batch system with throughput requirements. These have fundamentally different architectural constraints, and a common mistake is applying batch assumptions to the serving path or vice versa.

Feature stores have emerged as a critical architectural component. A feature store (like Feast, Tecton, or Uber's Michelangelo) provides a centralized repository for feature definitions, computation, and serving. It decouples feature engineering from model training and serving, allowing data scientists to define features once and use them across training and inference. The key architectural decision is the online vs offline feature serving split. Offline features are computed in batch for training. Online features must be computed in real-time for serving — often with sub-millisecond latency. Feature consistency — ensuring the same feature value is used in training and serving — is the single most common source of training-serving skew.

Model registries (like MLflow Model Registry, Google's Vertex AI Model Registry, or homegrown solutions) solve a different problem: model versioning, lineage tracking, and deployment management. Every model that goes to production must have a unique version, a pointer to the training run that produced it, the evaluation metrics, and a promotion status (staging → canary → production). The Staff+ engineer's concern is the deployment pipeline: canary deployment for ML models (start with 1% of traffic, gradually increase), automatic rollback on metric degradation, and shadow mode evaluation (run the new model alongside the old one without serving its predictions).

sections.title: Working with ML Engineers and Data Scientists

description.value: The Staff+ engineer working with ML teams faces a partnership challenge that is different from working with pure software engineers. ML engineers and data scientists think in probabilities, not certainties. They are comfortable with systems that are 'good enough' (95% accuracy, 80% recall) rather than systems that are 'correct.' They iterate through experiments rapidly and expect infrastructure that can support fast prototyping. The Staff+ systems engineer's job is to provide a platform that enables this iteration without sacrificing production reliability.

The most common friction point is the 'model in a notebook' problem. A data scientist develops a model in a Jupyter notebook with 100MB of sampled data. The model achieves 92% accuracy. The data scientist wants to deploy to production. The Staff+ engineer knows that a notebook-trained model on sampled data will fail in production for reasons the data scientist has not considered: training-serving skew, feature computation differences, data distribution shifts, latency constraints, and scalability limits. The solution is not to say no — it is to build a bridge. Provide a standardized training pipeline that converts notebook code to production-ready training jobs. Provide feature engineering libraries that ensure consistency between training and serving. Provide deployment templates that handle canary, rollback, and monitoring automatically.

Translating business problems to ML problems is where Staff+ engineers add unique value. A product manager says: 'We want to reduce customer churn.' An ML engineer will immediately think about building a churn prediction model. A Staff+ engineer will ask: 'Is churn prediction the right problem, or should we build a recommendation system that increases engagement? Is the data available for prediction? What is the action we take on prediction? If we predict churn, do we have a retention workflow to execute?' This systems-level thinking — connecting ML predictions to business actions — is what prevents ML projects from being technically successful but business-null. For SAP engineers, this framing is natural because SAP consulting is fundamentally about connecting technical solutions to business outcomes.

sections.title: MLOps and ML Infrastructure at FAANG

description.value: MLOps (Machine Learning Operations) at FAANG is a mature discipline with established patterns. Model deployment follows a staged rollout: shadow mode (model runs but predictions are logged, not served), canary (model serves 1-5% of traffic while being monitored), staged rollout (gradually increase to 25%, 50%, 100%), and full production. At each stage, automated validation gates check for metric degradation. If AUC drops by >1% or latency increases by >10ms, the rollout auto-rolls back. The Staff+ engineer designs these gates and the pipeline that connects model registry to deployment infrastructure.

Monitoring model drift is the hardest operational problem in ML systems. Unlike traditional software, an ML system can be operating correctly from an infrastructure perspective (no errors, low latency, high throughput) while producing completely wrong predictions. Data drift occurs when the input data distribution changes from the training distribution. Concept drift occurs when the relationship between input and output changes. Both require automated detection and response. The standard approach is to compute distribution statistics (mean, variance, percentiles) for every feature in every prediction batch and compare against a baseline using statistical tests (Kolmogorov-Smirnov test, Population Stability Index). When drift is detected, the system should alert and optionally trigger automatic retraining.

A/B testing for ML systems adds another layer of complexity. When you A/B test a new model against the old one, you need to ensure that (a) the traffic split is consistent, (b) metrics are computed on the same population, (c) the experiment has enough statistical power, and (d) you account for interference between the two model groups (network effects, market dynamics). FAANG companies have dedicated experimentation platforms (Google's Overlapping Experiment Infrastructure, Meta's PlanOut, Amazon's WebLab) that handle these complexities. As a Staff+ engineer, you need to understand these platforms well enough to integrate your ML system with them and interpret experiment results.

Large Language Models (LLMs) introduced a new paradigm that differs from traditional ML systems in fundamental ways. Traditional ML models are specialized (one model predicts one thing). LLMs are general — they can reason, summarize, generate, and classify. But they also introduce new failure modes: hallucination, prompt injection, output bias, and cost volatility. As a Staff+ engineer building with LLMs, you are designing systems that constrain LLM behavior through retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), prompt engineering, guardrails, and evaluation frameworks.

RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) is the dominant LLM integration pattern at FAANG. Instead of relying on the LLM's training data (which is static and may lack domain-specific knowledge), RAG retrieves relevant documents from a knowledge base and includes them in the LLM prompt as context. The architecture: a document ingestion pipeline (chunking, embedding, indexing into a vector database), a retrieval service (query embedding + vector similarity search), and a generation service (prompt construction with retrieved context + LLM call). The key engineering challenges: chunking strategy (document splitting that preserves semantic meaning), embedding model selection (bilingual support if serving multilingual users), retrieval latency (must be <100ms for real-time applications), and context window management (trimming retrieved documents to fit the LLM's context length).

Cost considerations dominate LLM system design. Each LLM call costs money ($0.01-$0.10 per million tokens depending on model), and at FAANG scale (millions of users), these costs add up fast. The Staff+ engineer's toolkit for cost optimization: caching (cache LLM responses for identical inputs), semantic caching (cache responses for similar inputs using embedding similarity), prompt compression (reduce token count without losing information), model routing (use small/cheap models for simple tasks, large/expensive models for complex ones), and fallback chaining (try a cheap model first, escalate to expensive model if confidence is low). Evaluation is equally critical — LLMs do not have a single accuracy metric. You need task-specific evaluation: answer correctness, faithfulness (did the model hallucinate?), relevance, and safety. Build automated eval sets before deploying to production.

SAP engineers bring a unique and undervalued perspective to ML systems at FAANG: deep understanding of structured, governed, transactional data. The ML industry is currently grappling with data quality problems that SAP systems solved decades ago. SAP's master data management (MDM) ensures that customer, product, and vendor records are consistent across the enterprise. SAP's data governance frameworks provide lineage tracking, access control, and audit trails. SAP's transactional model (ACID compliance, two-phase commit, idempotency) ensures data integrity. Most ML data infrastructure lacks these properties — and it shows.

The specific areas where SAP engineers can bridge to ML: (1) Feature engineering for structured data — SAP engineers understand time-series data, categorical hierarchies, and relational data models better than ML engineers who think in unstructured text and images. Your ability to design features from ERP data (order patterns, inventory levels, supply chain metrics) is directly applicable to ML systems that predict demand, optimize logistics, or detect anomalies. (2) Data quality monitoring — SAP engineers have spent years debugging data issues in migration projects. The same instincts apply to detecting training-serving skew, feature drift, and data pipeline failures in ML systems. (3) Governance and compliance — As ML regulation evolves (GDPR for ML, model risk management frameworks), SAP engineers who understand compliance (SOX, HIPAA, GDPR) are invaluable in designing ML systems that meet regulatory requirements.

To build your ML bridge: start with the ML pipeline end-to-end. Take one production ML system at your FAANG (your team's or a neighboring team's) and trace the entire flow: data ingestion → feature computation → training → evaluation → deployment → monitoring. Map each component to systems concepts you already understand. Data ingestion is an ETL pipeline with reliability requirements. Feature computation is a materialized view with consistency requirements. Model training is a batch job with resource management requirements. Model serving is a low-latency API with caching and load balancing. Model monitoring is observability with statistical alerting. Once you see ML systems as distributed systems with probabilistic components, you stop being intimidated by ML and start being effective at ML systems thinking.

value.title: The ML Skeptic's Trap
value.scenario: You are a Staff+ infrastructure engineer on a team building an ML-powered recommendation system. You believe the team should focus on rule-based heuristics instead, because ML models are black boxes with unpredictable failure modes. Your ML engineer colleague disagrees strongly.

bad.label: Bad Approach: Block ML Adoption
bad.text: Insisting that all problems should be solved with deterministic systems signals that you have not evolved beyond traditional software engineering. At FAANG, ML is not optional — it is the default approach for recommendation, ranking, personalization, and optimization. Blocking ML adoption will marginalize you from the product's direction. You will become the 'legacy infrastructure' engineer, maintaining systems that are being replaced.

good.label: Good Approach: Hybrid Architecture with Guardrails
good.text: Propose a hybrid: 'Let's use ML for the core ranking, but build a rule-based overlay that constrains outputs within safety bounds. The ML model proposes top-10 recommendations; the rule layer ensures we never recommend out-of-stock items, never recommend competitor products, and always include at least 2 new options.' Design the ML component so it can be validated, monitored, and rolled back independently. If you need ML to meet latency SLOs, build the infrastructure to make it reliable. Your skepticism is valuable — channel it into building robust systems around the ML, not against it.

details: Staff+ Insight: You do not need to be an ML engineer to be a Staff+ engineer. But you need to be ML-literate. Every major FAANG product now has ML components, and Staff+ engineers who cannot design around ML will find their scope limited. Start with understanding the ML pipeline end-to-end, then specialize in one area.
